You have just read the final chapter of Judges, and you should feel the weight of how far Israel has fallen. What began as a nation called to be God's holy people has descended into a moral free fall so complete that the narrator must step back and explain it: "In those days there was no king in Israel; everyone did what was right in his own eyes." This is not a neutral observation. It is an indictment. The people of God have become lawless, and the lawlessness has metastasized into something grotesque—the systematic slaughter of an entire city, followed by the sanctioned abduction of dancing girls to solve the problem the slaughter created. The book does not ask you to admire their problem-solving or their loyalty to oaths. It asks you to see what happens when a people abandons the Word of God and begins to construct their own morality.

Notice what the people grieve over. They do not grieve over the massacre of Jabesh-gilead—an entire city of men, women, and children put to the sword because they missed an assembly. They do not grieve when they orchestrate the kidnapping of four hundred young women at a religious festival. Their sorrow is reserved for the political problem: Benjamin might cease to exist as a tribe. They want to preserve the nation's structure, even as they abandon the nation's God. This is the essence of spiritual blindness. They perform religious acts—building altars, making offerings, weeping before the LORD—while committing acts that violate everything God's law actually teaches about the sanctity of life and the protection of the vulnerable. Their religion has become a mask for their rebellion.

The real tragedy is not that they lack a king—it is that they have rejected the King they already possess. God gave Israel His law, His covenant, His presence. At every turn in this final chapter, they chose to honor their own oaths and solve their own problems rather than repent and seek God's wisdom. They bound themselves with vows made in anger, then compounded the evil by trying to keep those vows through more evil. They did not ask whether breaking an oath sworn in sin might be the righteous path. They did not ask God what to do. Instead, they engineered a solution that left them feeling they had preserved something, when in truth they had preserved only the appearance of Israel while gutting it of justice, mercy, and godliness. You are reading this account not to learn how to govern a nation, but to recognize yourself—how easily you rationalize, how quickly you construct religious justifications for doing what is right in your own eyes, how often you treat obedience to your own plans as more sacred than obedience to God.
